coredata.dc:title: Concentration-modulated dual-excitation fluorescence of carbon dots used for ratiometric sensing of Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Bin
Zhou, Xiao-Qin
Lin, Jun-Ming
Liu, Shu-Yu
Lu, Cong-Hua
Liu, Li-Juan
Chen, Yu
Yu, Li-Ping
coredata.dc:description: The development of ratiometric fluorescence sensing platforms with simple synthetic routes and good anti-interference capacities are of great importance for their further application. Herein, hyperbranched polyethylenimine passivated carbon dots (HPEI-CDs) with concentration-modulated dual-excitation fluorescence property were synthesized. At a low concentration, HPEI-CDs only showed one fluorescence center, while at a relatively high concentration, its excitation peak around 355 nm would split into two peaks. Energy level splitting and broadening were demonstrated to be the origin of such dual-excitation behavior. Based on the dual-excitation signals, HPEI-CDs could serve as a ratiometric sensor for the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution. The assay exhibited a wide linear response concentration range (2–360 μM) with a limit of detection of 0.47 μM. Significantly, benefiting from the high concentration of HPEI-CDs, the sensor showed good selectivity and excellent anti-interference capacity. In addition, this nanosensor was successfully applied for the detection of Fe3+ in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Ratiometric sensor
Excitation spectra
Ferric ion

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of HPEI passivated carbon dots (HPEI-CDs)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Preparation route, (B) TEM image, (C) FT-IR spectra, (D) XPS spectra and (E) 1H NMR spectrum of HPEI-CDs.
2D fluorescence maps of HPEI-CDs aqueous solutions at different concentration (A: 0.03 mg mL−1, B: 0.1 mg mL−1, C: mg mL−1, D: 1 mg mL−1, E: 3 mg mL−1, F: 10 mg mL−1).
(A, B) The fluorescence excitation spectra, (C) Tauc plots of HPEI-CDs solutions at different concentration, (D) a possible energy level diagram of HPEI-CDs.
Effect of (A) temperature, (B) pH and (C) ionic strength on the dual-excitation property of HPEI-CDs (the concentration of HPEI-CDs is 1 mg mL−1).

(A) Fluorescence excitation spectra of HPEI-CDs at various concentration of Fe3+ ranging from 0 to 0.9 mM, (B) Linear relationship of the ratiometric fluorescence intensity I321/I382 versus Fe3+ concentration.
Fluorescence response of HPEI-CDs aqueous solution in the presence of various (A) metal ions and (B) anions.

head.ce:title: Concentration-modulated dual-excitation fluorescence of carbon dots used for ratiometric sensing of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Science, Tianjin Chengjian University, Tianjin 300384, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Science, Tianjin Chengjian University, Tianjin 300384, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300354, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300354, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tianjin Chengjian University, Tianjin 300384, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Disaster Medicine, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Disaster Medicine, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300072, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The development of ratiometric fluorescence sensing platforms with simple synthetic routes and good anti-interference capacities are of great importance for their further application. Herein, hyperbranched polyethylenimine passivated carbon dots (HPEI-CDs) with concentration-modulated dual-excitation fluorescence property were synthesized. At a low concentration, HPEI-CDs only showed one fluorescence center, while at a relatively high concentration, its excitation peak around 355 nm would split into two peaks. Energy level splitting and broadening were demonstrated to be the origin of such dual-excitation behavior. Based on the dual-excitation signals, HPEI-CDs could serve as a ratiometric sensor for the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution. The assay exhibited a wide linear response concentration range (2–360 μM) with a limit of detection of 0.47 μM. Significantly, benefiting from the high concentration of HPEI-CDs, the sensor showed good selectivity and excellent anti-interference capacity. In addition, this nanosensor was successfully applied for the detection of Fe3+ in real water samples with satisfactory recoveries.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence sensors, which can provide reliable fluorescence response to specific analytes, have attracted great attention due to their advantages of excellent resolution, noninvasiveness, technical simplicity and good sensitivity [1,2]. Up to now, they have been widely used in the sensing of biomolecules [3], intracellular pH [4], metal ions [5], anions [6], toxic pollutants [7], etc. Normally, the interactions between the sensors and the targets either reduce the fluorescence intensity (turn-off model) [8,9], or enhance it (turn-on model) [10]. However, these two manners using the fluorescence intensity at a wavelength to quantitatively analyze the target can sometimes be problematic, since the interference arising from instrumental parameters, photobleaching, microenvironment around the probe and working temperature can always affect the analysis. One effective way to overcome these drawbacks is to adopt the so-called ratiometric approach. Ratiometric fluorescence assay is a sensing approach based on the ratio changes of the fluorescence intensity at two or more wavelengths, and the effective introducing of internal reference greatly improves the accuracy. Until now, a large number of ratiometric sensors have been reported [11,12]. However, there are still some problems that impede their further application. First, the preparation processes of ratiometric sensors were usually complex and time consuming [13,14]. Second, in order to improve the sensing sensitivity, these sensors usually worked at very dilute conditions, which in turn reduced their anti-interference capacity [9,15]. Therefore, it is of great importance to development new ratiometric sensing platforms to solve these problems.

Carbon dots (CDs), as one of the newly developed members of carbon materials, possess many outstanding properties including low toxicity, chemical inertness, cheapness, good photostability and water solubility [16]. Due to these advantages, many researchers paid their attention to fabricate ratiometric sensors based on CDs in recent years [6,17–21]. The most conventional method is to hybridize CDs with other fluorescent substances, such as fluorescent dyes and metal nanoclusters [14,22–24]. For instance, Tian et al. synthesized a class of dansyl chloride modified CDs, which showed two emission peaks at ~412 nm and 500 nm, respectively. The PL intensity at 500 nm could respond to Fe3+, while the PL intensity at 412 nm remained constant [25]. In addition, CDs with dual-emission behavior could be also used as ratiometric sensor [26,27]. Pang et al. prepared a nitrogen doped CDs, which displayed two fluorescent emission peaks at 470 and 570 nm, respectively, and the fluorescence at 570 nm could be quenched solely by Fe3+, thus the ratiometric sensing platform was realized [28]. It is noteworthy that fluorescence sensors ever reported were mostly based on their emission signals, whereas other fluorescence information that can be acquired by using spectrofluorimeter such as excitation and light scattering signals were always ignored [29].

Fe3+ is one of the most important transition metals in organisms and nature, and it plays indispensable roles in many physiological processes. Thus, the accurate determination of Fe3+ concentration in water sample is not only meaningful for human health but also for environmental safety. Recently, a few works reported the concentration-modulated fluorescence properties of some specific CDs [30–38], which aroused our interest. In this research, we successfully prepared a hyperbranched polyethylenimine passivated CDs. Interestingly, the as-prepared CDs showed concentration-modulated dual-excitation behavior in certain concentration ranges. Based on this novel property, it could serve as a ratiometric sensor for the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution with comparable limit of detection (LOD) and good selectivity. Compared with other previous reported ratiometric sensors, such HPEI-CDs dual-excitation sensing platform showed many advantages. First, the probe could be easily constructed by just regulating the concentration of HPEI-CDs, which was quite simple and convenient. Second, this sensing system worked at a concentrated condition, which was in favor of masking the weak interactions between interfering substances and the probes, and endowed it excellent anti-interference capacity. Additionally, to the best of our knowledge, ratiometric sensors based on fluorescence excitation signals have never been reported. We expect that this new way of utilizing fluorescence can open a new gate for the constructing of ratiometric fluorescence sensor.

Hyperbranched polyethylenimine (HPEI, Mn = 1.8 × 103 g mol−1) was purchased from Alfa Aesar. Citric acid (CA), Na2HPO4 and NaOH were obtained from Tianjin Yuanli Chemical Company. HgCl2 was purchased from Xiya Reagent (Sichuan China). FeCl3·6H2O and other metal salts were purchased from Tianjin Jiangtian Chemical Company and Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Company.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was recorded by a JEOL JEM-2100F instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed with an ESCALBA Xi+ photoelectron spectrometer. The 1H NMR spectrum was recorded by a Bruker 400 MHz spectrometer. Hitachi F7000 photoluminescence spectrometer was used to record the fluorescence spectra. The UV–Vis spectrum was recorded by a Purkinje General T6 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. Avatar FT-IR360 (Nicolet) was used to record the infrared spectra of the samples. The lifetime of the prepared CDs was acquired by a Fluorolog 3 (HOR1RA JOB1N YVON). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurement were performed using Anton Paar Litesizer 500.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of HPEI passivated carbon dots (HPEI-CDs)
ce:section.ce:para: A typical synthesis procedure was as follows : 0.5 g of CA and 1.5 g of HPEI were dissolved in 10 mL of de-ionized water to form a transparent solution. The mixture was transferred to a 25-mL Teflon-line stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered by 0.22-μm film and dialyzed in water for 10 h. After lyophilizing, the yellow solid product was re-dissolved in water with a final concentration of 200 mg mL−1 and stored at 4 °C.

The detection of Fe3+ by using HPEI-CDs was performed at room temperature in 0.1 M CA-Na2HPO4 buffer at pH 5.5. HPEI-CDs was diluted with buffer solution to a final concentration of 1.4 mg mL−1, and Fe3+ solutions with various concentrations were prepared by dissolving FeCl3 in CA-Na2HPO4 buffer (0.1 M, pH 5.5). For the detection procedures, equal amount of HPEI-CDs and Fe3+solutions were mixed at room temperature. After incubation for 5 min, the changes of fluorescence excitation spectra were recorded at the emission wavelength of 455 nm. In addition, the selectivity of the sensing system was conducted by mixing 14 metal ions or 15 anions aqueous solutions with equal amount of HPEI-CDs buffer solution, and the final concentration of ions and HPEI-CDs were 0.25 mM and 0.7 mg mL−1, respectively. After being incubated for 5 min, the fluorescence excitation spectra were recorded. These detection procedures were performed in triplicate.

Tap water and lake water samples were collected from our laboratory and Mingfei Lake in Tianjin Chengjian University, respectively. All the samples were filtered with 0.45-μm filter before use. For the detection procedure, water samples were spiked with various concentration of standard Fe3+ solutions, and then mixed with equal amount of HPEI-CDs buffer solutions. Then the fluorescence excitation spectra were recorded.

Nitrogen doped CDs have been widely studied due to their high quantum yield, good water solubility and biocompatibility [39,40]. In this contribution, HPEI passivated CDs was prepared according to the literature with a slight modification (Fig. 1 A) [41]. The morphology, structure and composition of the synthesized CDs were characterized. Fig. 1B shows the TEM image of HPEI-CDs, it is clear that HPEI-CDs are spherical in shape, and most particles are observed to be amorphous carbon particles without lattices. They display a narrow size distribution in the range of 3.5–7 nm, and the maximum size population is around 5 nm. Fig. 1C depicts the FT-IR spectra of basic HPEI, CA and the prepared HPEI-CDs, respectively. HPEI-CDs shows similar characteristic absorption bands of HPEI, such as –NH of 3423 and 1557 cm−1, –CH2 of 2953 and 2841 cm−1, but nearly no characteristic absorption of CA. These results indicate that HPEI is quite stable during the pyrolysis process, while CA has been carbonized completely. In addition, a new absorption peak associated with amide linkage (–CONH–) around 1652 cm−1 demonstrates that abundant HPEI segments have been grafted onto the surface of the resultant CDs.

The chemical composition and structure of HPEI-CDs were further analyzed by XPS and NMR. The XPS spectrum (Fig. 1D) confirms that HPEI-CDs is composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen, and the atomic percentages of C, N and O atoms are determined to be 69.4%, 17.3%, and 9.6%, respectively. The high-resolution XPS survey of C1s can be de convoluted into three contributions, corresponding to graphitic or aliphatic, nitrous, and oxygenated carbon atoms at 284.1, 285.2 and 287.3 eV, respectively [42]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum (Fig. S1) at binding energies of 398.9 and 399.8 eV can be easily identified, which are attributed to the formation of CN–H and N–H, respectively [43]. The O1s peaks at 531.1 and 533.6 eV indicate the formation of C–O/C–O–C, whereas the peak at 532.2 eV is attributed to oxygenated carbon atoms (Fig. S2) [4]. These results indicate that the as-prepared HPEI-CDs are rich in hydrophilic groups on the surface. Fig. 1E shows the 1H NMR of HPEI-CDs. The strongest signals located at 2.30–2.90 ppm are attributed to the methylene units of HPEI segments. Furthermore, the signals coming from carbon-carbon double bond are founded at 5.86 and 5.98 ppm, indicating that sp2-hybrid carbon atoms are generated during the hydrothermal process. Moreover, it can be seen that almost all the peaks from CA vanish, implying that CA has been decomposed completely. This is in good consistent with FT-IR spectra.

The as-prepared HPEI-CDs showed excellent water solubility. No visible aggregates could be found even at the concentration up to 200 mg mL−1. When being irradiated by a UV lamp, its dilute solution showed bright blue fluorescence (Fig. S3). Its optical properties at the concentration of 0.04 mg mL−1 were investigated and shown in Fig. S4. According to the UV–vis absorption spectrum, an absorption band centered at 355 nm is observed, which can be ascribe to the n-π* transition of heteroatom (N and O) containing bonds [28,44]. The fluorescence spectrum displays an maximum emission at 445 nm at the excitation of 355 nm. Moreover, the quantum yield (QY) of HPEI-CDs was determined to be 42.1% by using quinine sulfate as the standard.

To investigate the concentration-dependent fluorescence property of HPEI-CDs, 2D fluorescence matrix scans were performed. Fig. 2 A–E shows the 2D fluorescence maps of HPEI-CDs aqueous solution at the concentration of 0.03, 0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, and 10 mg mL−1, respectively. In Fig. 2A, one symmetric ellipse fluorescence center is observed within the excitation range of 340–370 nm, which can be ascribed to the intrinsic fluorescence sites of HPEI-CDs. With the concentration increase, the ellipse fluorescence center gradually becomes flat and elongated. The maximum emission wavelength remains unchanged (~445 nm), while the maximum excitation wavelength is broadened to the range of 325–385 nm at the concentration of 0.3 mg mL−1 (Fig. 2C). Interestingly, with the further concentration increasing, the fluorescence center splits into two centers (Fig. 2D, E), and these two centers are gradually separated. The outcome revels that the concentration of HPEI-CDs plays an important role in its fluorescence excitation behavior.

In the subsequent study, the excitation fluorescence spectra of HPEI-CDs at different concentrations were recorded. When the concentration is below 0.18 mg mL−1 (Fig. 3 A), increasing concentration only results in the increase of fluorescence intensity, while the maximum excitation wavelength remains constant (~355 nm). When the concentration is higher than 0.375 mg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity declines gradually with the concentration increase, which can be attributed to the self-absorption and inner filter effect of HPEI-CDs at concentrated condition. More visibly, the excitation peak around 355 nm splits into two peaks in the concentration range of 0.375–12.0 mg mL−1 (Fig. 3B). With the concentration increase, one peak shifts from 355 to 290 nm, while the other peak shifts from 355 to 422 nm. The distance and the intensity ratio of these two peaks can be easily modulated by changing the concentration (Table S1). This indicates that HPEI-CDs can transform from single excitation system to dual-excitation system by varying its concentration to a certain range. It is noteworthy that, though a few works have concerned the effect of concentration on the fluorescence properties of CDs in recent years [30–36], this concentration-dependent dual-excitation phenomenon has rarely been reported.

To clarify the mechanism of the concentration-dependent dual-excitation property of HPEI-CDs, its absorption spectra at different concentrations were measured (Fig. S5), and then converted to Tauc plots of (αhυ)2 verses hυ to determine the optical gaps (Eg ), where α, h and υ are the absorption coefficient, Plank constant and light frequency, respectively. In the Tauc plots (Fig. 3C), the intersection values on the x-axe are Eg , and their values are listed in Table S2. When the concentration of HPEI-CDs is below 0.16 mg mL−1, its Eg is ~3.17 eV, while a further increasing of concentration results in the decrease of Eg . For instance, the Eg value is reduced to 2.98 eV when the concentration is 10 mg mL−1. This result implies that a rearrangement of the energy-level of HPEI-CDs occurs with the concentration variation. In addition, the emission spectra of HPEI-CDs show perceptible red-shift with the concentration increase (Fig. S6), and the life-time of HPEI-CDs at the concentrated condition are also prolonged from 9.01 to 12.05 ns (Table S3, τ 2). These results further support our deduction above. Moreover, the dispersion states of HPEI-CDs at different concentration were monitored by DLS. The outcome reveals that a slight aggregation occurs with the concentration increase (Fig. S7). According to the quantum confinement effect, energy level splitting and broadening occur in CDs aggregation process and will cause a reduction of the band gap [45]. Therefore, as the increase of HPEI-CDs concentration, on the one hand, the Eg value declines gradually, and on the other hand, the excited state energy levels within the n-π* gap also split and broaden, which makes some excited energy levels easily get into the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital levels. Thus, the concentration-induced dual-excitation property is generated. A possible energy level diagram is shown in Fig. 3D.

According to the above achievements, HPEI-CDs could show dual-excitation fluorescence behavior at certain concentrations. Subsequently, the stability of this property was investigated. The concentration of HPEI-CDs was fixed at 1 mg mL−1, and then the fluorescence excitation spectra at different temperature were recorded (Fig. 4 A). There is no significant change in the overall shape of the fluorescence spectra upon raising temperature from 25 to 85 °C, except the slight decrease of fluorescence intensity. It is reasonable that high temperature is beneficial to increase the mobility of the functional groups on HPEI-CDs, and the non-radiative transition is enhanced, which results in the decrease of fluorescence intensity. The spectral information is further dissected and the ratios of fluorescence intensity at 315 nm and 395 nm (I1/I2) are displayed in the inset of Fig. 4A. It is clear that the value of I1/I2 almost remains constant, indicating that temperature cannot affect the ratio of the intensity of these two peaks. Fig. 4B shows the fluorescence excitation spectra of HPEI-CDs at different pHs. The fluorescence intensity changes a lot at different pHs, and the two maximum fluorescence excitation peaks shift slightly, while the intensity ratio of these two peaks only shows a negligible changing (~1.48 ± 0.02) in the pH range of 1–7. The stability of HPEI-CDs under high ionic strength was also investigated. Different amounts of NaCl were added to HPEI-CDs solutions, and their fluorescence excitation spectra were shown in Fig. 4C. It is found that the fluorescence spectra are independent in the presence of 0 up to 0.5 M NaCl, and the values of I1/I2 remain 1.43 in all the range. In addition, the fluorescence excitation spectra of HPEI-CDs are almost unchanged after shelf-storage for 30 days (Fig. S8). Overall, all these experiments demonstrate that the dual-excitation property of HPEI-CDs is quite stable, and the intensity ratio of these two peaks is insensitive to the environmental change, which makes it great potential for the further application.

Encouraged by the novel concentration-dependent dual-excitation property of HPEI-CDs, its application as ratiometric fluorescence sensor for specific analyte was explored. Herein, the concentration of HPEI-CDs was fixed at 0.7 mg mL−1. At this concentration, HPEI-CDs showed two isolated excitation peaks at 321 nm and 382 nm, respectively. When different amount of Fe3+ ions were added to the solution, its fluorescence was quenched (Fig. S9), while the quenching degrees at 321 nm and 382 nm were different. Figs. S10 and 5 A show the change of fluorescence excitation spectra of HPEI-CDs solution after incubation with various concentration of Fe3+ ions. The calibration plot of the ratio of the fluorescence intensities (I321/I382) as a function of Fe3+ concentration is shown in Fig. 5B. The signals of I321/I382 are proportional toward Fe3+ concentrations from 2.0 μM to 360 μM with a correlation coefficient of 0.998 and equation y = 0.0015x + 1.33. The LOD determined by 3δ/S (δ is the standard deviation of the lowest signal and S is the slop of linear calibration plot) is 0.47 μM, which is comparable with other CDs probes for Fe3+ sensing (Table S4) and much lower than the limit of Fe3+ in drinking water (~5.357 μM) set by the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency [46]. This indicates that this dual-excitation system can be applied as a sensitive ratiometric probe for Fe3+ detection in aqueous solution.

Selectivity is another important parameter for sensing application. Herein, the selectivity of HPEI-CDs towards Fe3+ was evaluated through two comparative experiments. First, 0.25 mM different types of metal ions were incubated with HPEI-CDs, respectively. The value of I321/I382 were calculated according to their fluorescence excitation spectra and shown in Fig. 6 A. Of all these ions, only Fe3+ and Cu2+ can change the value of I321/I382 dramatically. The influence of binary mixture of Fe3+ and other metal ions on I321/I382 were also examined. The addition of other metal ions except Cu2+ do not influence the quenching effect of Fe3+. Secondly, the interference of anions on the detection of Fe3+ were detected. The outcome reveals that I321/I382 is independent in the presence of these anions. It is noteworthy that, of all these interfering ions except Cu2+, the changes of I321/I382 caused by them are all less than 0.8%, which is only ~2.6% of the changing caused by Fe3+. When compared with other CDs sensing systems which always worked at very dilute condition, this concentrated HPEI-CDs sensing system showed much better anti-interference capacity (Table S5) [44]. We conjecture that the high concentration of HPEI-CDs is in favor of masking the weak interaction between CDs and the interfering substances, which results in the excellent anti-interference capacity.

As stated above, Cu2+ is a non-negligible interfering substrate for Fe3+ sensing, thus, how to further improve the specificity is critical. In the following experiment, HPEI-CDs was dissolved in CA/Na2HPO4 buffer at pH 2.6, and the sensing procedure was carried out. Fig. S11 shows the fluorescence excitation spectra under this condition. In the absence of metal ions, I324/I384 of HPEI-CDs is 1.31. When Fe3+ is added, the fluorescence is quenched, and the value of I324/I384 decreases to 0.91, which is close to the value obtained at pH 5.5. When Cu2+ is added to HPEI-CDs solution, the fluorescence spectrum does not change too much, and the value of I321/I382 is 1.29. This is because a more acidic condition is conductive to reduce the affinity of polyamine segments on HPEI-CDs to Cu2+, which in turn lead to the reduced changing of fluorescence spectrum. Therefore, Fe3+ and Cu2+ can be effectively distinguished by performing the sensing at pH 2.6.

To elucidate the sensing mechanism of HPEI-CDs to Fe3+, the UV absorption spectra of HPEI-CDs and Fe3+, and the excitation spectrum of HPEI-CDs were studied. As shown in Fig. S12, Fe3+ exhibits a broad absorption peak from 250 to 425 nm, and shows good spectra overlapping with HPEI-CDs. Therefore, we conclude that the inner filter effect (IFE) is the main reason for the fluorescence quenching of HPEI-CDs by Fe3+. What’s more, the absorbance of Fe3+ declines gradually with the increasing of wavelength. For instance, its absorbance at 321 nm was 4.9 times higher than at 382 nm, which would result in the different quenching degrees of HPEI-CDs by Fe3+ at different excitation wavelength. Thus, the ratiometric sensing is realized.

Due to the excellent selectivity and sensitivity for Fe3+ detection, the proposed ratiometric sensor was assessed to detect Fe3+ in real water samples, including tap water and lake water. As the concentration of Fe3+ in tap water and lake water was extremely low, it could not be detected. We then employed the standard addition method for Fe3+. As shown in Table S6, the quantitative spike recovery values are in the range of 96.4%–104.4% with relative standard deviations (RSD) bellow 4.3% for each sample. These results indicate that this ratiometric fluorescence sensor has great potential for the detection of Fe3+ ions in environmental water samples.

Fluorescent HPEI-CDs were prepared via hydrothermal method successfully. It showed a novel concentration-dependent dual-excitation fluorescence behavior. When the concentration of HPEI-CDs was lower than 0.18 mg mL−1, its fluorescence intensity increased with the concentration increase, while the maximum excitation and emission wavelength remained constant. When the concentration exceeded 0.18 mg mL−1, the fluorescence excitation peak would split into two peaks, and the distance and intensity ratio of these two peaks could be easily modulated by regulating the concentration. Energy level splitting and broadening were demonstrated to be the origin of this dual-excitation behavior. What’s more, the dual-emission property of HPEI-CDs was quite stable at different temperature and pH, and also tolerant to ionic strength and storage time. Based on this novel fluorescence behavior, HPEI-CDs was able to serve as ratiometric fluorescence sensor for the detection of Fe3+ in aqueous solution with excellent anti-interference capacity, good LOD and selectivity. Compared with other previously-reported sensing methods, this HPEI-CDs dual-excitation sensing platform showed many advantages. First, the preparation method of this sensor is quite simple and convenient, and no subsequent chemical modification is required. Secondly, the high concentration of HPEI-CDs endows this assay excellent anti-interference capacity. Additionally, ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on excitation signals is rare, we expect that this strategy may offer a new approach for the development of this field.

Bin Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Xiao-Qin Zhou: Data curation. Jun-Ming Lin: Data curation. Shu-Yu Liu: Data curation. Cong-Hua Lu: Resources. Li-Juan Liu: Resources. Yu Chen: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Li-Ping Yu: Conceptualization, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [21875159, 21976130, 51703154] and Tianjin Natural Science Foundation [18JCYBJC23400].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106028.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Small molecule-based ratiometric fluorescence probes for cations, anions, and biomolecules

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.H. Lee, J.S. Kim, J.L. Sessler, Small molecule-based ratiometric fluorescence probes for cations, anions, and biomolecules, Chem. Soc. Rev., 44(2015) 4185-4191.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on nanostructured carbon quantum dots and their applications in biotechnology, sensors, and chemiluminescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.J. Molaei, A review on nanostructured carbon quantum dots and their applications in biotechnology, sensors, and chemiluminescence, Talanta, 196(2019) 456-478.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tetraphenylethene decorated with disulfide functionalized hyperbranched poly(amido amine)s as metal/organic solvent-free turn-on AIE probes for biothiol determination

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Wang, C. Li, L. Yang, C. Zhang, L.-J. Liu, S. Zhu, Tetraphenylethene decorated with disulfide functionalized hyperbranched poly(amido amine)s as metal/organic solvent-free turn-on AIE probes for biothiol determination, J. Mater. Chem. B, 7(2019) 3846-3855.
